units.title: Digital Literacy
units.description: In this unit, students develop foundational digital literacy skills by learning to confidently and responsibly use common digital tools and systems. Through practical, hands-on activities, students explore Microsoft Word, PowerPoint and Excel to create, manage and communicate information using appropriate conventions. The unit also introduces key concepts in cybersecurity, digital footprints and emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, helping students understand how data is created, shared and protected in networked environments. Across the term, students build skills in selecting suitable digital tools, managing files and data, and using technology safely and ethically, preparing them for further learning in Digital Technologies and other learning areas.

assessments.title: Digital Tasks
assessments.description: Students will create a digital portfolio that demonstrates their learning across the term through a collection of completed tasks and reflections. The portfolio includes evidence of using word processing, presentation and spreadsheet tools to create, organise, analyse and communicate information, as well as activities focused on cybersecurity awareness, safe online behaviour and responsible use of AI tools. Students are expected to select and use digital tools efficiently, apply common conventions, manage data accurately, and demonstrate an understanding of how information is shared and secured online. The portfolio provides ongoing evidence of skill development, responsible digital practices and the ability to reflect on learning progress over time, aligned to the Year 7–8 Digital Technologies standards.

units.title: Wearable Technology
units.description: In this unit, students use Micro:bit devices to explore data, communication and physical computing in a practical and age-appropriate context. Students learn how Microbits collect, transmit and represent data, with a focus on radio communication, binary representation and basic data security concepts. Through guided investigations, students explore how data can be gathered from sensors, transmitted between devices and analysed to identify patterns and trends. In the second part of the unit, students apply these skills to design and create wearable technologies, combining computational thinking, data analysis and ethical considerations when collecting data about people.

assessments.title: Data Exam
assessments.description: In this assessment, students complete a written exam that assesses their understanding of how Microbits communicate and represent data. Students explain the differences between broadcast and direct radio communication, and demonstrate their understanding of how text, numbers and messages are represented using integers and binary. The exam also assesses students’ ability to model and reason about structured data, design and trace basic algorithms, and predict program outputs related to Microbit communication and simple encryption. This assessment provides evidence of students’ conceptual understanding of data transmission, representation and algorithmic thinking.

assessments.title: Wearable Tech
assessments.description: In this assessment, students design and develop a wearable Microbit-based solution to collect and analyse data related to a given investigation task. Students plan how data will be gathered using appropriate sensors, design algorithms to collect and store the data, and use digital tools to analyse and visualise results to draw conclusions. They select suitable hardware, design the user experience, manage the project process and evaluate their solution against design criteria and user needs. Students also demonstrate responsible and ethical data collection practices, reflecting on the purpose and impact of their digital solution.

units.title: Robotics
units.description: In this unit, students investigate the relationship between hardware specifications and system performance using mBot robots. They apply systems thinking to explain how specific hardware components and sensor capabilities affect a robot's functionality, selecting appropriate hardware for particular tasks and workloads. Students apply computational thinking to decompose real-world problems, representing algorithmic solutions through flowcharts and pseudocode before implementing them in a programming environment. This involves the use of control structures, including selection and repetition, to process sensor input and automate physical movement. Through divergent design thinking techniques, students generate and review ideas against established design criteria and user stories to ensure solutions meet specific needs. The unit is framed around a search-and-rescue mission, requiring students to iteratively test and debug their robotic solutions to ensure they meet safety and performance specifications.

assessments.title: MBot Project
assessments.description: Students design and develop an autonomous robotic solution using the MBot platform to navigate a complex course. They apply systems thinking to explain how hardware specifications and sensor capabilities affect performance, justifying their selection of specific components for the required navigation tasks. Using computational thinking, students decompose the problem into logical components and represent their solutions through flowcharts and pseudocode before implementation in a general-purpose programming language. The project requires students to iteratively test and debug their algorithms, evaluating the final prototype against design criteria and user stories to ensure the hardware effectively supports the intended performance workloads and functional requirements.
